draft.introduction: Chrysippus (c. 279–206 BCE), the third head of the Stoa, systematized Stoic determinism. His doctrine of fate holds that all events are linked in an unbreakable causal chain determined by the divine Logos, while attempting to preserve moral responsibility.

translation.original_ref: SVF II.913–914 (Aulus Gellius, Noctes Atticae VII.2.1–6)
translation.text: All things happen according to fate. Fate is a certain natural ordering of all things, following one upon another from eternity and intertwined in an unalterable chain. Chrysippus uses the analogy of a cylinder rolling down a hill: the push is the external cause, but the manner of rolling belongs to the cylinder’s own nature.

translator_notes.note: The cylinder analogy attempts to reconcile determinism with moral responsibility: while external circumstances initiate action, the agent’s character determines the response. This “compatibilist” position influenced centuries of debate on free will and fate.
